news_summary: Recent discoveries reveal natural processes mitigating carbon emissions, enhanced understanding of animal cooperation, innovative urban wildlife support, and insights into longevity in butterflies.
news_summary_fr: Des découvertes récentes mettent en lumière des processus naturels permettant de réduire les émissions de carbone, une meilleure compréhension de la coopération animale, des initiatives innovantes en faveur de la faune urbaine, ainsi que des éclairages sur la longévité des papillons.
news_summary_es: Los últimos descubrimientos revelan procesos naturales que mitigan las emisiones de carbono, una mejor comprensión de la cooperación entre animales, iniciativas innovadoras de apoyo a la fauna urbana y nuevos conocimientos sobre la longevidad de las mariposas.

articles.title: Hidden geological process offsets carbon emissions from thawing permafrost
articles.summary: Scientists found that thawing permafrost can trigger increased rock weathering, a natural process that absorbs CO2 from the atmosphere. In some regions, this carbon uptake was strong enough to fully offset — or even surpass — river greenhouse gas emissions.
articles.content:
A study published in Nature suggests that thawing permafrost may have an unexpected effect on the carbon cycle. While melting frozen ground is widely known for releasing greenhouse gases, researchers found that it can also strengthen a natural process that removes carbon dioxide (CO 2 ) from the atmosphere.

The research was conducted by scientists from Umeå University in Sweden and East China Normal University in China.

Thawing Permafrost Can Increase CO 2 Removal

As global temperatures rise, permafrost is thawing across many regions, exposing long-frozen organic matter. Microbes break down this ancient carbon and release greenhouse gases, leading many scientists to view thawing permafrost primarily as a growing source of emissions.

However, the new study points to another process taking place at the same time. As frozen ground degrades, previously buried minerals become exposed and water interacts more extensively with rock surfaces. These changes accelerate chemical weathering, a process that can consume atmospheric CO 2 .

According to the researchers, this weathering-driven carbon uptake can significantly reduce the amount of CO 2 released by rivers. In some cases, it can even completely offset those emissions.

To investigate the phenomenon, the team studied 50 rivers across the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, the world's largest high-altitude cryosphere outside the polar regions. They analyzed river CO 2 emissions, dissolved carbon, isotopic tracers, and geochemical models to better understand how thawing permafrost affects carbon cycling.

Their results showed that thawing landscapes enhance chemical weathering, moving carbon into dissolved inorganic forms while simultaneously removing CO 2 from the atmosphere.

Carbon Uptake Can Surpass River Emissions

"We found that river CO 2 emissions decline while carbon uptake through rock weathering increases as permafrost cover decreases," said Liwei Zhang, biogeochemist at East China Normal University. "In some catchments where permafrost has become patchier, weathering-driven carbon uptake was large enough to offset or even exceed river CO 2 emissions."

Across the study area, the researchers estimated that rock weathering offsets about 35 percent of river CO 2 emissions on average.

The effect varied depending on the extent of permafrost coverage. Areas with continuous permafrost showed only modest offsets. In contrast, regions with discontinuous or isolated permafrost sometimes experienced weathering-driven carbon uptake that exceeded 100 percent of river CO 2 emissions. These findings indicate that carbon removal through geological processes can, in some situations, rival carbon release from biological activity.

Geological and Biological Carbon Cycles Interact

The study challenges the idea that thawing permafrost functions only as a source of carbon emissions.

When frozen soils thaw, rivers receive large amounts of ancient organic carbon. Microorganisms then convert some of that material into greenhouse gases that enter the atmosphere. The new findings suggest that geological processes occurring alongside these biological processes may help offset part of those emissions.

The researchers emphasize that rock weathering should not be viewed as a simple or permanent solution to climate change. Carbon cycling in thawing environments is highly complex, and some weathering reactions can release CO 2 depending on the minerals involved.

Instead, the study highlights an important mechanism that is not fully represented in many climate and carbon cycle models.

"Our findings show that biological and geological carbon cycles are tightly linked," says Jan Karlsson, professor at Department of Ecology, Environment and Geoscience at Umeå University. "To understand whether thawing permafrost ultimately amplifies or dampens climate warming, we need to consider both the carbon released from ancient soils and the carbon consumed through rock weathering."

The researchers say future climate assessments should look beyond biologically driven carbon emissions alone and account for geological sources and sinks that emerge as frozen landscapes continue to thaw.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that thawing permafrost triggers increased rock weathering, a natural process that absorbs CO2 and can offset or surpass greenhouse gas emissions from rivers. This finding reveals a previously underappreciated natural carbon sink with broad implications for understanding and modeling climate change, offering a hopeful insight into natural processes that may mitigate some effects of global warming.
articles.personality_title: Thawing permafrost speeds up natural process that absorbs carbon dioxide
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Permafrost is frozen soil found in cold places like the Arctic. When it thaws because of warmer temperatures, it usually releases gases that trap heat in the atmosphere, which can speed up climate change.

**What happened** – Scientists from Sweden and China studied 50 rivers on the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau. They discovered that thawing permafrost also exposes rocks to water, causing a natural process called chemical weathering. This process pulls carbon dioxide (CO2) out of the air and stores it in the water, which can reduce the amount of CO2 released by rivers.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows thawing permafrost doesn’t only release CO2 but can also help remove it from the atmosphere. In some areas, the carbon absorbed through rock weathering was enough to completely cancel out or even exceed the CO2 that rivers released. This means that natural geological processes can balance some of the emissions caused by thawing permafrost.

**What's next step** – Researchers say future climate studies should include both the carbon released by microbes and the carbon absorbed by weathering. Understanding this balance better will help improve climate models and predictions about how thawing permafrost affects global warming.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Thawing permafrost speeds up rock weathering, a natural process that can absorb enough carbon dioxide to offset greenhouse gas emissions from rivers in some regions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le dégel du pergélisol accélère un processus naturel d’absorption du dioxyde de carbone
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le pergélisol est un sol gelé que l’on trouve dans les régions froides comme l’Arctique. Lorsqu’il dégèle à cause du réchauffement, il libère souvent des gaz qui retiennent la chaleur dans l’atmosphère, ce qui peut accélérer le changement climatique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de Suède et de Chine ont étudié 50 rivières sur le plateau Qinghai-Tibet. Ils ont découvert que le dégel du pergélisol expose aussi des roches à l’eau, provoquant un processus naturel appelé altération chimique. Ce processus retire le dioxyde de carbone (CO2) de l’air et le stocke dans l’eau, ce qui peut réduire la quantité de CO2 libérée par les rivières.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela montre que le dégel du pergélisol ne libère pas seulement du CO2, mais peut aussi aider à l’éliminer de l’atmosphère. Dans certaines zones, le carbone absorbé par l’altération des roches suffisait à annuler ou même dépasser le CO2 libéré par les rivières. Cela signifie que des processus géologiques naturels peuvent compenser une partie des émissions liées au dégel.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs disent que les futures études climatiques devraient prendre en compte à la fois le carbone libéré par les microbes et celui absorbé par l’altération. Mieux comprendre cet équilibre aidera à améliorer les modèles climatiques et les prévisions sur l’impact du dégel du pergélisol sur le réchauffement global.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le dégel du pergélisol accélère l’altération des roches, un processus naturel capable d’absorber suffisamment de dioxyde de carbone pour compenser les émissions de gaz à effet de serre des rivières dans certaines régions.
articles.personality_title_es: El deshielo del permafrost acelera un proceso natural que absorbe dióxido de carbono
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El permafrost es suelo congelado que se encuentra en lugares fríos como el Ártico. Cuando se descongela por el aumento de temperaturas, suele liberar gases que atrapan el calor en la atmósfera, lo que puede acelerar el cambio climático.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de Suecia y China estudiaron 50 ríos en la meseta Qinghai-Tíbet. Descubrieron que el deshielo del permafrost también expone rocas al agua, causando un proceso natural llamado meteorización química. Este proceso extrae dióxido de carbono (CO2) del aire y lo almacena en el agua, lo que puede reducir la cantidad de CO2 que liberan los ríos.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que el permafrost que se descongela no solo libera CO2, sino que también puede ayudar a eliminarlo de la atmósfera. En algunas áreas, el carbono absorbido por la meteorización fue suficiente para cancelar o incluso superar el CO2 liberado por los ríos. Esto significa que los procesos geológicos naturales pueden equilibrar parte de las emisiones causadas por el deshielo.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores dicen que los futuros estudios climáticos deben considerar tanto el carbono liberado por los microbios como el carbono absorbido por la meteorización. Entender mejor este equilibrio ayudará a mejorar los modelos climáticos y las predicciones sobre cómo el deshielo del permafrost afecta al calentamiento global.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El deshielo del permafrost acelera la meteorización de las rocas, un proceso natural que puede absorber suficiente dióxido de carbono para compensar las emisiones de gases de efecto invernadero de los ríos en algunas regiones.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a high-altitude river winding through thawing permafrost landscapes on the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, where exposed rocky riverbanks glow softly to symbolize accelerated chemical weathering absorbing carbon dioxide, with faint wisps of greenhouse gases gently rising and being balanced by mineral-rich waters flowing steadily, capturing the harmonious interplay of geological and biological carbon cycles in a serene natural setting.

articles.title: The secret language behind animal cooperation
articles.summary: Animals from different species often rely on surprisingly sophisticated communication to work together, whether finding food, cleaning parasites, or gaining protection. New research suggests these interspecies “conversations” are flexible, evolved, and far more important to life in nature than scientists once realized.
articles.content:
Animals don't just communicate with members of their own species. New research shows that communication also plays a crucial role in helping different species work together.

In a review published in Animal Behaviour, an international team of researchers examined how animals use calls, body movements, visual displays, and other signals to coordinate cooperative relationships across species. The findings reveal the many ways animals exchange information to synchronize their behavior and maintain partnerships that benefit both sides.

Different species can cooperate in surprisingly diverse situations. Some birds lead humans to bees' nests in exchange for access to beeswax. Cleaner fish remove parasites from larger reef fish and receive a meal in return. Drawing on examples from birds, fish, insects, and mammals, the researchers show how communication helps these relationships function and endure.

How Animals Coordinate Across Species

For cooperation to succeed, animals must often match the timing of their actions to achieve a shared goal. This can be especially challenging when the species involved perceive the world in different ways.

One example is the greater honeyguide bird (Indicator indicator), which uses specialized calls to attract humans and lead them to bees' nests. The bird also responds to calls made by humans. In another case, warthogs use distinctive body postures to invite birds and mammals to clean them.

"From the examples we know, individuals coordinate their actions to access shared resources, like food, or to exchange resources for services, such as protection from predators," said Dr. Katie Dunkley, lead author and researcher at the University of Oxford. "We were particularly interested in how sharing information allows such close coordination between species."

Communication Helps Manage Risks

Signals and behavioral cues do more than initiate cooperation. They also help animals identify trustworthy partners and reduce the risk of being exploited.

Interactions with another species can be beneficial, but they can also be dangerous. Communication allows animals to distinguish between partners that offer a genuine service and those that might take advantage of them.

For instance, some cleaner fish (e.g. Labroides dimidiatus) and shrimp (e.g. Urocaridella sp.) display bright colors and perform distinctive movements that signal their role to predatory fish, allowing cleaning interactions to occur safely. Likewise, lycaenid butterfly larvae use chemical and vibrational signals that encourage ants to protect them rather than eat them.

The review also highlights the importance of looking beyond visual signals. Many species rely on information gathered through multiple senses, suggesting that scientists may overlook important forms of communication if they focus only on what animals can see.

Flexible and Adaptable Signals

Not all communication systems are the same. Some remain highly consistent, while others vary depending on location and environmental conditions.

Fish seeking cleaning services often use predictable postures, such as headstands or tail stands. In contrast, fishermen working with dolphins may interpret different dolphin behaviors as cues for when to cast their nets, and those signals can vary from one region to another.

"In some forms of interspecies cooperation, cues and signals vary depending on the ecological context, the species involved, and whether the signal is inherited or learned," said senior author Dr. van der Wal, a researcher affiliated with UCT's FitzPatrick Institute of African Ornithology. "This highlights just how flexible and adaptable interspecies communication can be."

How Cross-Species Communication Evolves

The researchers also explored how communication systems between species may develop over time.

Some signals may begin as simple cues, meaning traits or behaviors that influence another animal's response even though they were not originally meant to communicate information. Over generations, those cues can become more specialized signals.

Other communication signals may start out serving entirely different purposes, such as helping animals care for offspring or resolve conflicts. Eventually, those behaviors can be adapted for use in cooperative interactions between species.

"Studying how information flows between species gives us a powerful window into how communication systems originate, change and sometimes coevolve," said Dr. Dunkley.

Large Collaborative Research Effort

The review grew out of an interdisciplinary workshop focused on interspecies cooperation that took place in Cambridge in July 2023. Researchers studying a wide range of systems participated in the event.

The resulting paper includes 58 authors from multiple fields, including anthropology, biology, and linguistics. It also brings together experts studying animal cooperation across species, mixed-species interactions, and systems in which humans actively train non-human animals.

What Scientists Hope to Learn Next

According to the researchers, the review highlights promising new directions for studying how communication evolves between species and how these interactions influence ecosystems.

The authors emphasize the need for broader studies involving more groups of animals, along with additional experiments aimed at understanding how signals arise, persist, and shape cooperative behavior.

"We still have much to learn about how these systems function and evolve," said Dr. van der Wal. "We look forward to future research revealing both these interactions and other forms of interspecies cooperation yet to be discovered."

The study was published in Animal Behaviour under the title, "The ecology and evolution of cues and signals in animal interspecies cooperation."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on new scientific research revealing sophisticated communication and cooperation between different animal species, highlighting evolved behaviors that benefit ecosystems. This has broad significance for understanding biodiversity, species interactions, and ecosystem health, providing meaningful insight into nature's complexity and potential conservation benefits.
articles.personality_title: New research reveals how animals talk to cooperate across species
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Animals don’t just communicate with their own kind. Scientists have found that different species also use signals like calls, movements, and colors to work together in nature.

**What happened** – An international team of researchers reviewed many examples of animal cooperation where communication helps. For instance, the greater honeyguide bird uses special calls to lead humans to bee nests, and cleaner fish signal larger fish to remove parasites. These signals help animals coordinate actions, share resources, and stay safe.

**Impact** – This research shows that animal communication across species is flexible and evolved, not just simple signals. Animals use different senses and adapt their signals depending on the situation. Understanding this helps explain how animals form partnerships that improve survival and maintain healthy ecosystems.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to study more animals and test how these communication systems develop and change over time. They hope to discover new types of cooperation and learn how these interactions affect the environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Animals use complex and flexible communication to cooperate across species, revealing new insights into how nature works together.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle recherche révèle comment les animaux communiquent pour coopérer entre espèces
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les animaux ne communiquent pas seulement avec les membres de leur propre espèce. Les scientifiques ont découvert que différentes espèces utilisent aussi des signaux comme des appels, des mouvements et des couleurs pour travailler ensemble dans la nature.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe internationale de chercheurs a passé en revue de nombreux exemples de coopération animale où la communication joue un rôle clé. Par exemple, l’oiseau guide-honey utilise des appels spéciaux pour conduire les humains aux nids d’abeilles, et les poissons nettoyeurs signalent aux gros poissons de leur enlever les parasites. Ces signaux aident les animaux à coordonner leurs actions, partager des ressources, et rester en sécurité.

**Impact** – Cette recherche montre que la communication entre espèces est souple et évoluée, pas seulement un simple signal. Les animaux utilisent différents sens et adaptent leurs signaux selon la situation. Comprendre cela aide à expliquer comment les animaux forment des partenariats qui améliorent leur survie et maintiennent des écosystèmes en bonne santé.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent étudier plus d’animaux et tester comment ces systèmes de communication se développent et changent avec le temps. Ils espèrent découvrir de nouveaux types de coopération et comprendre comment ces interactions influencent l’environnement.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les animaux utilisent une communication complexe et adaptable pour coopérer entre espèces, révélant de nouvelles clés sur le fonctionnement de la nature.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva investigación revela cómo los animales se comunican para cooperar entre especies
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los animales no solo se comunican con otros de su misma especie. Los científicos han descubierto que diferentes especies también usan señales como llamadas, movimientos y colores para trabajar juntas en la naturaleza.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo internacional de investigadores revisó muchos ejemplos de cooperación animal donde la comunicación es clave. Por ejemplo, el pájaro guía de miel usa llamadas especiales para llevar a los humanos a los nidos de abejas, y los peces limpiadores señalan a peces más grandes para que les quiten parásitos. Estas señales ayudan a los animales a coordinar sus acciones, compartir recursos y mantenerse seguros.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación muestra que la comunicación entre especies es flexible y ha evolucionado, no es solo una señal simple. Los animales usan diferentes sentidos y adaptan sus señales según la situación. Entender esto ayuda a explicar cómo los animales forman asociaciones que mejoran la supervivencia y mantienen ecosistemas saludables.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren estudiar más animales y probar cómo estos sistemas de comunicación se desarrollan y cambian con el tiempo. Esperan descubrir nuevos tipos de cooperación y entender cómo estas interacciones afectan al medio ambiente.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los animales usan una comunicación compleja y adaptable para cooperar entre especies, revelando nuevas ideas sobre cómo funciona la naturaleza.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a greater honeyguide bird softly calling to a silhouette of a human figure, both surrounded by a vibrant natural scene where a cleaner fish performs a graceful tail stand near a larger reef fish, symbolizing interspecies communication and cooperation through gentle, natural colors and harmonious interaction.

articles.title: The tiny highway helping the capital's hedgehogs
articles.summary: A hedgehog highway is a network of small holes and gaps that allows hedgehogs to move freely.
articles.content: Kate Scott-Gatty, a researcher at ZSL's Institute of Zoology, said: "Hedgehogs have declined historically throughout Britain and they're now on the red list for British mammals as vulnerable to extinction and we've also seen that decline in London as well.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a conservation effort, the creation of a hedgehog highway, which helps a vulnerable species move freely and potentially recover population numbers. This has meaningful environmental impact and benefits biodiversity in urban areas.
articles.personality_title: London’s hedgehog highway helps protect a vulnerable species
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Hedgehogs are small animals that have been disappearing across Britain, including London. They are now considered vulnerable to extinction, meaning their numbers are very low and at risk.

**What happened** – A special network called the hedgehog highway has been created in London. This highway is made up of small holes and gaps in fences and walls that let hedgehogs move safely between gardens and parks.

**Impact** – This highway helps hedgehogs find food, mates, and new homes more easily. By connecting different green spaces, it supports the survival and growth of hedgehog populations in the city.

**What’s next step** – Researchers and local people will keep building and improving these highways. They plan to monitor how the hedgehogs use them to understand better ways to protect the animals.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Small pathways in London’s gardens are helping hedgehogs move freely and survive in the city.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’autoroute miniature de Londres aide à protéger les hérissons vulnérables
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les hérissons sont de petits animaux qui disparaissent à travers la Grande-Bretagne, y compris à Londres. Ils sont maintenant considérés comme vulnérables à l’extinction, ce qui signifie que leur nombre est très faible et en danger.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un réseau spécial appelé l’autoroute pour hérissons a été créé à Londres. Cette autoroute est composée de petits trous et espaces dans les clôtures et murs qui permettent aux hérissons de se déplacer en toute sécurité entre les jardins et les parcs.

**Impact** – Cette autoroute aide les hérissons à trouver de la nourriture, des partenaires et de nouveaux abris plus facilement. En reliant différents espaces verts, elle soutient la survie et la croissance des populations de hérissons en ville.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs et les habitants continueront à construire et améliorer ces autoroutes. Ils prévoient de surveiller comment les hérissons les utilisent pour mieux protéger ces animaux.

**Phrase clé** – De petits passages dans les jardins londoniens aident les hérissons à se déplacer librement et à survivre en ville.
articles.personality_title_es: La pequeña autopista de Londres ayuda a proteger a los erizos vulnerables
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los erizos son animales pequeños que están desapareciendo en todo Reino Unido, incluyendo Londres. Ahora se consideran vulnerables a la extinción, lo que significa que su número es muy bajo y está en riesgo.

**Qué pasó** – Se creó en Londres una red especial llamada autopista para erizos. Esta autopista está formada por pequeños agujeros y espacios en cercas y muros que permiten a los erizos moverse con seguridad entre jardines y parques.

**Impacto** – Esta autopista ayuda a los erizos a encontrar comida, pareja y nuevos hogares con mayor facilidad. Al conectar diferentes espacios verdes, apoya la supervivencia y crecimiento de las poblaciones de erizos en la ciudad.

**Próximo paso** – Investigadores y vecinos seguirán construyendo y mejorando estas autopistas. Planean observar cómo las usan los erizos para saber cómo proteger mejor a estos animales.

**Frase para recordar** – Pequeños caminos en los jardines de Londres están ayudando a los erizos a moverse libremente y sobrevivir en la ciudad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a small, cozy hedgehog highway bridge made of natural wood and leaves, gently arching over a quiet urban garden pathway at dusk, with several hedgehogs happily crossing beneath soft amber streetlights, surrounded by simple green shrubs and subtle London cityscape silhouettes in the background.

articles.title: A long-lived butterfly’s secret to graceful ageing
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 19 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01948-0Species in the Heliconius genus are among the longest-lived butterflies, thanks to a diet of pollen.
articles.content:
Species in the Heliconius genus are among the longest-lived butterflies, thanks to a diet of pollen.

Most tropical butterflies survive in their colourful adult forms for just a few weeks — the epitome of ephemeral beauty — but one group has evolved to live much longer. Jessica Foley at the University of Bristol, UK, and her colleagues studied the Heliconius butterfly genus, which 12 million–18 million years ago began adding pollen to butterflies’ typical nectar diet. Pollen provides the adults with nutrients that they can use to keep their bodies working longer and allows for prolonged reproduction. Data collated from published field studies and public butterfly houses show that at least one pollen-eating Heliconius hewitsoni individual lived to the venerable age of 348 days.

doi: https://doi.org/10.1038/d41586-026-01948-0

References Foley, J. et al. Nature Commun. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-73635-7 (2026). Download references

Subjects
articles.reasoning: The article reports a scientific discovery explaining the biological mechanism behind the unusually long lifespan of Heliconius butterflies due to their pollen diet. This insight contributes to understanding species longevity and ecosystem biodiversity, which has broad significance for environmental science and conservation.
articles.personality_title: How pollen helps some butterflies live nearly a year
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Most tropical butterflies live only a few weeks after becoming adults. Their bright colors and short life make them symbols of quick beauty in nature.

**What happened** – Scientists studied a group of butterflies called Heliconius, which started eating pollen along with nectar 12 to 18 million years ago. Unlike other butterflies, these Heliconius butterflies get extra nutrients from pollen that help their bodies last longer. One butterfly of this group was recorded to live 348 days, almost a full year.

**Impact** – This discovery explains why Heliconius butterflies live much longer than most others. It shows how a change in diet can affect an animal’s lifespan and ability to reproduce for a longer time. This helps scientists understand more about how animals age and survive in nature.

**What's next step** – Researchers can now explore how the nutrients in pollen work inside the butterflies’ bodies. This might offer clues about aging in other animals or ways to protect butterfly species that are important for ecosystems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Eating pollen allows Heliconius butterflies to live much longer than most butterflies by giving them nutrients that support a longer, healthier life.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment le pollen aide certains papillons à vivre presque un an
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La plupart des papillons tropicaux vivent seulement quelques semaines après être devenus adultes. Leurs couleurs vives et leur courte vie en font des symboles de beauté éphémère dans la nature.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont étudié un groupe de papillons appelé Heliconius, qui a commencé à manger du pollen en plus du nectar il y a 12 à 18 millions d’années. Contrairement aux autres papillons, ces Heliconius obtiennent des nutriments supplémentaires du pollen qui aident leur corps à durer plus longtemps. Un papillon de ce groupe a été enregistré vivant 348 jours, presque une année entière.

**Impact** – Cette découverte explique pourquoi les papillons Heliconius vivent beaucoup plus longtemps que la plupart des autres. Elle montre comment un changement d’alimentation peut influencer la durée de vie et la capacité à se reproduire plus longtemps. Cela aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre le vieillissement et la survie des animaux dans la nature.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs peuvent maintenant étudier comment les nutriments du pollen agissent dans le corps des papillons. Cela pourrait donner des indices sur le vieillissement chez d’autres animaux ou des moyens de protéger les papillons importants pour les écosystèmes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Manger du pollen permet aux papillons Heliconius de vivre beaucoup plus longtemps que la plupart des papillons en leur fournissant des nutriments qui soutiennent une vie plus longue et plus saine.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo el polen ayuda a algunas mariposas a vivir casi un año
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La mayoría de las mariposas tropicales viven solo unas pocas semanas después de convertirse en adultas. Sus colores brillantes y vida corta las hacen símbolos de belleza efímera en la naturaleza.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos estudiaron un grupo de mariposas llamado Heliconius, que comenzó a comer polen junto con néctar hace 12 a 18 millones de años. A diferencia de otras mariposas, estas Heliconius obtienen nutrientes extra del polen que ayudan a que sus cuerpos duren más tiempo. Se registró un individuo que vivió 348 días, casi un año completo.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento explica por qué las mariposas Heliconius viven mucho más que la mayoría. Muestra cómo un cambio en la dieta puede afectar la duración de vida y la capacidad de reproducirse más tiempo. Esto ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor cómo envejecen y sobreviven los animales en la naturaleza.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores pueden ahora explorar cómo funcionan los nutrientes del polen dentro del cuerpo de las mariposas. Esto podría dar pistas sobre el envejecimiento en otros animales o formas de proteger mariposas importantes para los ecosistemas.

**Frase clave** – Comer polen permite a las mariposas Heliconius vivir mucho más tiempo que la mayoría al darles nutrientes que apoyan una vida más larga y saludable.
